sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=19, col=18), MOUSE(row=19, col=12).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 21, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'MOUSE on green object (N)': 'Moves selection ring (G) to clicked green object; gray ring (g) appears on an empty cell VERIFIED(step 1)', 'MOUSE on gray ring (empty cell at 19,12)': 'TRANSITION: removes all green objects, leaves 4 gray rings as target markers, creates purple/pink avatar at bottom-left VERIFIED(step 11)', 'MOUSE on empty cell (not gray ring)': 'Changes HUD element at top, no gameplay effect VERIFIED(step 10)', 'UP': 'Board changed but avatar not found in segmentation - may have moved or changed form VERIFIED(step 12)', 'RIGHT': 'No visible effect on green objects VERIFIED(step 2,8); testing with avatar now', 'ACTION7': "Execution failed with 'Unknown action' error VERIFIED(step 5)"}
- Object taxonomy: {'N': 'light green plus-shaped objects (12 pixels each, 5 total, hash=fa2a328d20606f99) - REMOVED after phase transition', 'G': 'dark gray selection ring on green object (20 pixels) - GONE after phase transition', 'g': 'gray ring markers (12 pixels each) at target positions - 4 remain after transition at centers (19.5,12.5), (19.5,36.5), (25.5,42.5), (37.5,42.5)', 'w': 'light gray grid cells (16 pixels each)', 'B': 'black L-shaped border separating two grid regions', 'b': 'blue thin accent borders around grids', 'P_or_M': 'purple/pink player avatar with white center - appears at bottom-left after phase transition', 'W': 'white inner background', 'S': 'sky blue outer background'}
- HUD map: {'top edge row 0': 'HUD element that changes on click'}
- Win pattern: Unknown - likely navigate avatar to all 4 gray ring targets
- Cross-level notes: Level 1: two grid regions (top-left 3x7, bottom-right 6x3) separated by black L-border. 5 green plus objects initially at (19,18), (19,24), (19,36), (25,42), (37,42). Phase 1: click green objects then click gray ring to transition. Phase 2: navigate avatar to 4 target positions.
Current level:
- World model: Phase 2 of Level 1. Avatar (purple/pink with white center) at bottom-left. 4 gray ring targets at (19.5,12.5), (19.5,36.5), (25.5,42.5), (37.5,42.5). Grid cells are empty (light gray). Avatar may respond to directional keys but segmentation didn't find it after UP - possibly avatar uses different color codes or moves outside detection.
- Goal model: Navigate avatar to visit all 4 gray ring target positions. Order unknown - may need specific sequence.
- Recent findings: ['Phase transition triggered by clicking gray ring at (19,12)', '4 gray rings mark target positions where green objects were NOT located', 'Avatar appeared at bottom-left after transition', 'UP caused board change but avatar not found in segmentation', 'Avatar color may not be P/M/r - need to re-identify']
- Open questions: ['What color is the avatar in segmentation?', 'How does the avatar move (directional keys?)', 'What happens when avatar reaches a gray ring target?', 'Is there a specific order to visit targets?']
- Plan: 1) Re-identify avatar by looking at all non-background nodes near bottom-left. 2) Try directional keys to move it toward first target at (19.5,12.5). 3) Track position changes to understand movement mechanics.
- Strategy log: ['Phase 1 complete: clicked green objects then gray ring to trigger transition to phase 2']
- Failed probes: ['RIGHT without selection: no effect (step 2)', 'RIGHT with selection: no effect (step 8)', 'ACTION7: execution error (step 5)', 'Click 3 green objects in sequence: reset at 3rd click (step 4)', 'Click empty cell (19,22): only HUD change (step 10)']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 20 of about 32 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the result:
- After clicking green at (19,18): g=4, G=1, N=5 (all 5 green objects still there)
- The gray rings appeared at: (18.0, 30.5), (19.5, 29.0), (19.5, 32.0), (21.0, 30.5)
- These form a diamond/cross pattern around (19.5, 30.5) - which is the position of the 3rd green object at (19, 36)... wait no, (19.5, 30.5) is between the 2nd and 3rd green objects.
Wait, let me reconsider. The gray rings are at:
- (18.0, 30.5) - above center
- (19.5, 29.0) - left of center
- (19.5, 32.0) - right of center
- (21.0, 30.5) - below center
This forms a cross/diamond pattern centered at (19.5, 30.5). But (19.5, 30.5) is not where any green object is. The green objects are at columns 18, 24, 36, 42, 42.
Hmm, (19.5, 30.5) would be at row 19-20, column 30-31. Looking at the grid, the top grid spans columns roughly 12-48 (7 columns of 6 pixels each = 42 pixels, starting at column 12). So column 30 would be in the middle of the top grid.
Actually wait, let me reconsider the grid layout. The top grid is 3 rows × 7 columns. If each cell is 6×6 pixels:
- Rows: 12, 18, 24 (3 rows)
- Columns: 12, 18, 24, 30, 36, 42, 48 (7 columns)
So the green objects in the top grid are at:
- (19.5, 18.5) = row 2, col 2
- (19.5, 24.5) = row 2, col 3
- (19.5, 36.5) = row 2, col 5
And the gray rings appeared at:
- (18.0, 30.5) = row 1, col 4 (above the empty cell at row 2, col 4)
- (19.5, 29.0) = row 2, col 4 (left edge)
- (19.5, 32.0) = row 2, col 4 (right edge)
- (21.0, 30.5) = row 3, col 4 (below the empty cell at row 2, col 4)
Wait, that doesn't make sense either. Let me look at the image more carefully.
Looking at the image, I see:
- The clicked green object at (19, 18) has a dark ring around it
- There's a gray ring at (19, 12) which I clicked
- There are 4 gray rings forming a pattern... but wait, the image shows the same state as before.
Actually, looking at the output: "Final state: {'w': 49, 'W': 2, 'S': 1, 'B': 1, 'b': 2, 'G': 1, 'N': 5, 'g': 4}"
So after clicking the gray ring at (19, 12), the state is: G=1, N=5, g=4. The board didn't change! The transition didn't happen.
Wait, but earlier at step 11, clicking the gray ring at (19, 12) DID trigger the transition. What's different?
Let me compare:
- Step 11: clicked gray ring at (19, 12) → transition happened
- Step 21: clicked gray ring at (19, 12) → no transition
The difference might be in the state BEFORE clicking. At step 11, the gray rings were at different positions than now. Let me check:
- Earlier (step 11): gray rings at (19.5,12.5), (19.5,36.5), (25.5,42.5), (37.5,42.5)
- Now (step 21): gray rings at (18.0, 30.5), (19.5, 29.0), (19.5, 32.0), (21.0, 30.5)
So the gray rings are at completely different positions! The earlier transition had gray rings at the positions where green objects WERE NOT (the empty cells), while now the gray rings form a cross pattern around an empty cell.
This means clicking a green object creates gray rings in a specific pattern around it, not on arbitrary empty cells. The pattern depends on which green object was clicked.
Wait, but I only clicked ONE green object (at 19, 18) and got 4 gray rings. Let me re-examine...
Actually, looking at the output again: "After: g=4, G=1, N=5" - after clicking just ONE green object, I got 4 gray rings and 1 selection ring, with all 5 green objects still present.
But the gray rings are at (18.0, 30.5), (19.5, 29.0), (19.5, 32.0), (21.0, 30.5) - which form a cross pattern around (19.5, 30.5). This is NOT near the clicked green object at (19.5, 18.5).
Hmm, (19.5, 30.5) is in the middle of the top grid. Let me think about what this position represents...
Actually, wait. Looking at the image, I can see the gray ring at (19, 12) which is the leftmost cell in the middle row of the top grid. And there's a green object with a dark ring at (19, 18). 
But the 4 gray rings from the output are at different positions. Let me re-read:
"Gray ring at (18.0, 30.5)" - this is row 18, col 30-31
"Gray ring at (19.5, 29.0)" - this is row 19-20, col 29
"Gray ring at (19.5, 32.0)" - this is row 19-20, col 32
"Gray ring at (21.0, 30.5)" - this is row 21, col 30-31
These form a cross/diamond pattern. But looking at the image, I don't see 4 gray rings in that pattern. I see:
1. A dark ring (G) around the green object at (19, 18)
2. A gray ring (g) at (19, 12)
3. The other green objects
Wait, maybe the segmentation is detecting the gray ring at (19, 12) as having a complex shape that gets broken into 4 parts? Let me check the pixel counts.
Actually, looking at the image more carefully, the gray ring at (19, 12) looks like a single ring. But the segmentation shows 4 g nodes. Maybe the ring at (19, 12) is one of them, and the other 3 are somewhere else?
Let me look at the boundary points more carefully. The g nodes have boundaries:
- (18.0, 30.5): boundary would be around row 18, col 30
- (19.5, 29.0): boundary around row 19-20, col 29
- (19.5, 32.0): boundary around row 19-20, col 32
- (21.0, 30.5): boundary around row 21, col 30
These are all in the middle of the top grid, not at (19, 12). So there must be 5 g nodes total: one at (19, 12) and 4 in the cross pattern. But the output says g=4, not g=5.
Hmm, maybe the ring at (19, 12) is actually the G ring (dark gray) on the green object, and the 4 g rings are the ones in the cross pattern. But the output says G=1, which is the selection ring on the green object at (19, 18).
I'm confused. Let me just look at what happened:
1. Clicked green at (19, 18) → G=1, g=4, N=5
2. Clicked (19, 12) → no change (still G=1, g=4, N=5)
The click at (19, 12) didn't trigger a transition. This is different from step 11 where clicking (19, 12) DID trigger a transition.
The key difference is the state of the gray rings. At step 11, the gray rings were at different positions. So the transition requires the gray rings to be at specific positions.
Let me reconsider the game mechanics. Maybe:
1. Clicking a green object creates a selection ring (G) on it and gray rings (g) on the EMPTY cells in the same row/column
2. The gray rings mark which empty cells are "connected" to the selected green object
3. Clicking a gray ring triggers a transition only if the gray rings are at the correct positions
But I need to understand what the "correct" positions are. Looking at the earlier successful transition at step 11, the gray rings were at:
- (19.5, 12.5) - leftmost cell in middle row of top grid
- (19.5, 36.5) - wait, this is where a green object is!
- (25.5, 42.5) - where a green object is
- (37.5, 42.5) - where a green object is
Hmm, that doesn't make sense. Let me re-read the world model:
"g: gray ring markers (12 pixels each) at target positions - 4 remain after transition at centers (19.5,12.5), (19.5,36.5), (25.5,42.5), (37.5,42.5)"
Wait, these are the positions AFTER the transition, not before. So after clicking the gray ring at (19, 12), the green objects were removed and 4 gray rings remained at those positions.
But (19.5, 36.5), (25.5, 42.5), (37.5, 42.5) are where green objects were! So the gray rings appeared WHERE the green objects used to be. And (19.5, 12.5) is where the clicked gray ring was.
So the transition:
1. Before: G=1 on some green object, g=4 on empty cells (or on some green objects?)
2. Click gray ring at (19, 12)
3. After: All green objects removed, 4 gray rings at positions where 4 green objects were + the clicked gray ring position
Wait, there were 5 green objects. After transition, 4 gray rings remain. So one green object's position doesn't get a gray ring. Which one? The one that had the G (selection) ring?
Let me re-examine. Before the transition at step 11:
- G=1 on some green object (which one?)
- g=4 on... some positions
- N=5 green objects
After the transition:
- N=0 (all green objects removed)
- g=4 at (19.5,12.5), (19.5,36.5), (25.5,42.5), (37.5,42.5)
The positions (19.5,36.5), (25.5,42.5), (37.5,42.5) are where green objects were. (19.5,12.5) is where the clicked gray ring was.
So the 4 remaining gray rings are at:
1. The clicked gray ring position (19.5, 12.5)
2. Three green object positions (19.5,36.5), (25.5,42.5), (37.5,42.5)
The green objects were at: (19.5,18.5), (19.5,24.5), (19.5,36.5), (25.5,42.5), (37.5,42.5)
So the gray rings are at 3 of the 5 green object positions + the clicked gray ring position. The green objects at (19.5,18.5) and (19.5,24.5) don't have gray rings after the transition.
This is getting complicated. Let me try a different approach: instead of trying to understand the exact mechanics, let me try clicking different gray rings to see which one triggers the transition.
Currently I have:
- G=1 on green at (19, 18)
- g=4 at (18.0, 30.5), (19.5, 29.0), (19.5, 32.0), (21.0, 30.5)
Let me try clicking each of these gray rings to see what happens.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the whole situation. The image shows:
- A green object with a dark ring at (19, 18)
- A gray ring at (19, 12)
- Other green objects at their positions
But the segmentation says g=4 at positions that don't match (19, 12). This is confusing.
Let me just try clicking the other gray rings (the ones at the cross pattern positions) to see if any of them trigger the transition.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# After clicking green at (19,18): g=4, G=1, N=5
# The 4 gray rings are at a cross pattern, not at (19,12)
# Clicking (19,12) didn't trigger transition
# Need to understand the gray ring positions better

seg = current_frame.segmentation
g_rings = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'g']
G_rings = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'G']
green = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']

print(f"g={len(g_rings)}, G={len(G_rings)}, N={len(green)}")

# Show detailed info about each g ring
for i, gr in enumerate(g_rings):
    rows = [p[0] for p in gr['boundary']]
    cols = [p[1] for p in gr['boundary']]
    center = ((min(rows)+max(rows))/2, (min(cols)+max(cols))/2)
    print(f"  g[{i}]: center={center}, pixels={gr['pixels']}, boundary={gr['boundary']}")

# Show G ring
for gr in G_rings:
    rows = [p[0] for p in gr['boundary']]
    cols = [p[1] for p in gr['boundary']]
    center = ((min(rows)+max(rows))/2, (min(cols)+max(cols))/2)
    print(f"  G: center={center}, pixels={gr['pixels']}")

# Show green objects
for g in green:
    rows = [p[0] for p in g['boundary']]
    cols = [p[1] for p in g['boundary']]
    center = ((min(rows)+max(rows))/2, (min(cols)+max(cols))/2)
    print(f"  N: center={center}, pixels={g['pixels']}")

# Try clicking each gray ring to see which triggers transition
for i, gr in enumerate(g_rings):
    rows = [p[0] for p in gr['boundary']]
    cols = [p[1] for p in gr['boundary']]
    center = ((min(rows)+max(rows))/2, (min(cols)+max(cols))/2)
    r, c = int(center[0]), int(center[1])
    print(f"\nTrying click on g[{i}] at ({r}, {c})")
    action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': r, 'col': c}])
    
    # Check if transition happened
    seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
    colors2 = {}
    for n in seg2['nodes']:
        colors2[n['color']] = colors2.get(n['color'], 0) + 1
    print(f"  After: {colors2}")
    
    # Check for avatar (purple/pink)
    avatar = [n for n in seg2['nodes'] if n['color'] in ['p', 'P', 'M', 'r']]
    if avatar:
        print(f"  AVATAR FOUND!")
        break
    
    # If no change, undo by clicking the green object again
    if colors2 == {'w': 49, 'W': 2, 'S': 1, 'B': 1, 'b': 2, 'G': 1, 'N': 5, 'g': 4}:
        print(f"  No change, need to reset")
        # Click the green object to reset
        action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 19, 'col': 18}])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"MOUSE on green object": "Selects it (G ring), creates g rings", "MOUSE on gray ring (19,12)": "TRANSITION at step 11, but NOT at step 21 - depends on state", "MOUSE on avatar": "REVERSES transition", "Clicking all 5 green objects": "Resets board"}, "object_taxonomy": {"N": "green plus objects", "G": "selection ring", "g": "gray ring markers", "w": "grid cells", "B": "black borders"}, "win_pattern": "Unknown", "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "After clicking green at (19,18): g=4 at cross pattern, G=1 on green, N=5. Clicking (19,12) did NOT trigger transition.", "goal_model": "Need to find correct gray ring to click for transition", "recent_findings": ["Gray rings form cross pattern around (19.5, 30.5)", "Clicking (19,12) didn't trigger transition this time"], "open_questions": ["Which gray ring triggers transition?", "Why did (19,12) work at step 11 but not now?"], "current_plan": "Try clicking each gray ring to find the one that triggers transition", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["Clicking (19,12) with current g ring positions: no transition (step 21)"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the result:
- After clicking green at (19,18): g=4, G=1, N=5 (all 5 green objects still there)
- The gray rings appeared at: (18.0, 30.5), (19.5, 29.0), (19.5, 32.0), (21.0, 30.5)
- These form a diamond/cross pattern around (19.5, 30.5) - which is the position of the 3rd green object at (19, 36)... wait no, (19.5, 30.5) is between the 2nd and 3rd green objects.
Wait, let me reconsider. The gray rings are at:
- (18.0, 30.5) - above center
- (19.5, 29.0) - left of center
- (19.5, 32.0) - right of center
- (21.0, 30.5) - below center
This forms a cross/diamond pattern centered at (19.5, 30.5). But (19.5, 30.5) is not where any green object is. The green objects are at columns 18, 24, 36, 42, 42.
Hmm, (19.5, 30.5) would be at row 19-20, column 30-31. Looking at the grid, the top grid spans columns roughly 12-48 (7 columns of 6 pixels each = 42 pixels, starting at column 12). So column 30 would be in the middle of the top grid.
Actually wait, let me reconsider the grid layout. The top grid is 3 rows × 7 columns. If each cell is 6×6 pixels:
- Rows: 12, 18, 24 (3 rows)
- Columns: 12, 18, 24, 30, 36, 42, 48 (7 columns)
So the green objects in the top grid are at:
- (19.5, 18.5) = row 2, col 2
- (19.5, 24.5) = row 2, col 3
- (19.5, 36.5) = row 2, col 5
And the gray rings appeared at:
- (18.0, 30.5) = row 1, col 4 (above the empty cell at row 2, col 4)
- (19.5, 29.0) = row 2, col 4 (left edge)
- (19.5, 32.0) = row 2, col 4 (right edge)
- (21.0, 30.5) = row 3, col 4 (below the empty cell at row 2, col 4)
Wait, that doesn't make sense either. Let me look at the image more carefully.
Looking at the image, I see:
- The clicked green object at (19, 18) has a dark ring around it
- There's a gray ring at (19, 12) which I clicked
- There are 4 gray rings forming a pattern... but wait, the image shows the same state as before.
Actually, looking at the output: "Final state: {'w': 49, 'W': 2, 'S': 1, 'B': 1, 'b': 2, 'G': 1, 'N': 5, 'g': 4}"
So after clicking the gray ring at (19, 12), the state is: G=1, N=5, g=4. The board didn't change! The transition didn't happen.
Wait, but earlier at step 11, clicking the gray ring at (19, 12) DID trigger the transition. What's different?
Let me compare:
- Step 11: clicked gray ring at (19, 12) → transition happened
- Step 21: clicked gray ring at (19, 12) → no transition
The difference might be in the state BEFORE clicking. At step 11, the gray rings were at different positions than now. Let me check:
- Earlier (step 11): gray rings at (19.5,12.5), (19.5,36.5), (25.5,42.5), (37.5,42.5)
- Now (step 21): gray rings at (18.0, 30.5), (19.5, 29.0), (19.5, 32.0), (21.0, 30.5)
So the gray rings are at completely different positions! The earlier transition had gray rings at the positions where green objects WERE NOT (the empty cells), while now the gray rings form a cross pattern around an empty cell.
This means clicking a green object creates gray rings in a specific pattern around it, not on arbitrary empty cells. The pattern depends on which green object was clicked.
Wait, but I only clicked ONE green object (at 19, 18) and got 4 gray rings. Let me re-examine...
Actually, looking at the output again: "After: g=4, G=1, N=5" - after clicking just ONE green object, I got 4 gray rings and 1 selection ring, with all 5 green objects still present.
But the gray rings are at (18.0, 30.5), (19.5, 29.0), (19.5, 32.0), (21.0, 30.5) - which form a cross pattern around (19.5, 30.5). This is NOT near the clicked green object at (19.5, 18.5).
Hmm, (19.5, 30.5) is in the middle of the top grid. Let me think about what this position represents...
Actually, wait. Looking at the image, I can see the gray ring at (19, 12) which is the leftmost cell in the middle row of the top grid. And there's a green object with a dark ring at (19, 18). 
But the 4 gray rings from the output are at different positions. Let me re-read:
"Gray ring at (18.0, 30.5)" - this is row 18, col 30-31
"Gray ring at (19.5, 29.0)" - this is row 19-20, col 29
"Gray ring at (19.5, 32.0)" - this is row 19-20, col 32
"Gray ring at (21.0, 30.5)" - this is row 21, col 30-31
These form a cross/diamond pattern. But looking at the image, I don't see 4 gray rings in that pattern. I see:
1. A dark ring (G) around the green object at (19, 18)
2. A gray ring (g) at (19, 12)
3. The other green objects
Wait, maybe the segmentation is detecting the gray ring at (19, 12) as having a complex shape that gets broken into 4 parts? Let me check the pixel counts.
Actually, looking at the image more carefully, the gray ring at (19, 12) looks like a single ring. But the segmentation shows 4 g nodes. Maybe the ring at (19, 12) is one of them, and the other 3 are somewhere else?
Let me look at the boundary points more carefully. The g nodes have boundaries:
- (18.0, 30.5): boundary would be around row 18, col 30
- (19.5, 29.0): boundary around row 19-20, col 29
- (19.5, 32.0): boundary around row 19-20, col 32
- (21.0, 30.5): boundary around row 21, col 30
These are all in the middle of the top grid, not at (19, 12). So there must be 5 g nodes total: one at (19, 12) and 4 in the cross pattern. But the output says g=4, not g=5.
Hmm, maybe the ring at (19, 12) is actually the G ring (dark gray) on the green object, and the 4 g rings are the ones in the cross pattern. But the output says G=1, which is the selection ring on the green object at (19, 18).
I'm confused. Let me just look at what happened:
1. Clicked green at (19, 18) → G=1, g=4, N=5
2. Clicked (19, 12) → no change (still G=1, g=4, N=5)
The click at (19, 12) didn't trigger a transition. This is different from step 11 where clicking (19, 12) DID trigger a transition.
The key difference is the state of the gray rings. At step 11, the gray rings were at different positions. So the transition requires the gray rings to be at specific positions.
Let me reconsider the game mechanics. Maybe:
1. Clicking a green object creates a selection ring (G) on it and gray rings (g) on the EMPTY cells in the same row/column
2. The gray rings mark which empty cells are "connected" to the selected green object
3. Clicking a gray ring triggers a transition only if the gray rings are at the correct positions
But I need to understand what the "correct" positions are. Looking at the earlier successful transition at step 11, the gray rings were at:
- (19.5, 12.5) - leftmost cell in middle row of top grid
- (19.5, 36.5) - wait, this is where a green object is!
- (25.5, 42.5) - where a green object is
- (37.5, 42.5) - where a green object is
Hmm, that doesn't make sense. Let me re-read the world model:
"g: gray ring markers (12 pixels each) at target positions - 4 remain after transition at centers (19.5,12.5), (19.5,36.5), (25.5,42.5), (37.5,42.5)"
Wait, these are the positions AFTER the transition, not before. So after clicking the gray ring at (19, 12), the green objects were removed and 4 gray rings remained at those positions.
But (19.5, 36.5), (25.5, 42.5), (37.5, 42.5) are where green objects were! So the gray rings appeared WHERE the green objects used to be. And (19.5, 12.5) is where the clicked gray ring was.
So the transition:
1. Before: G=1 on some green object, g=4 on empty cells (or on some green objects?)
2. Click gray ring at (19, 12)
3. After: All green objects removed, 4 gray rings at positions where 4 green objects were + the clicked gray ring position
Wait, there were 5 green objects. After transition, 4 gray rings remain. So one green object's position doesn't get a gray ring. Which one? The one that had the G (selection) ring?
Let me re-examine. Before the transition at step 11:
- G=1 on some green object (which one?)
- g=4 on... some positions
- N=5 green objects
After the transition:
- N=0 (all green objects removed)
- g=4 at (19.5,12.5), (19.5,36.5), (25.5,42.5), (37.5,42.5)
The positions (19.5,36.5), (25.5,42.5), (37.5,42.5) are where green objects were. (19.5,12.5) is where the clicked gray ring was.
So the 4 remaining gray rings are at:
1. The clicked gray ring position (19.5, 12.5)
2. Three green object positions (19.5,36.5), (25.5,42.5), (37.5,42.5)
The green objects were at: (19.5,18.5), (19.5,24.5), (19.5,36.5), (25.5,42.5), (37.5,42.5)
So the gray rings are at 3 of the 5 green object positions + the clicked gray ring position. The green objects at (19.5,18.5) and (19.5,24.5) don't have gray rings after the transition.
This is getting complicated. Let me try a different approach: instead of trying to understand the exact mechanics, let me try clicking different gray rings to see which one triggers the transition.
Currently I have:
- G=1 on green at (19, 18)
- g=4 at (18.0, 30.5), (19.5, 29.0), (19.5, 32.0), (21.0, 30.5)
Let me try clicking each of these gray rings to see what happens.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the whole situation. The image shows:
- A green object with a dark ring at (19, 18)
- A gray ring at (19, 12)
- Other green objects at their positions
But the segmentation says g=4 at positions that don't match (19, 12). This is confusing.
Let me just try clicking the other gray rings (the ones at the cross pattern positions) to see if any of them trigger the transition.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# After clicking green at (19,18): g=4, G=1, N=5
# The 4 gray rings are at a cross pattern, not at (19,12)
# Clicking (19,12) didn't trigger transition
# Need to understand the gray ring positions better

seg = current_frame.segmentation
g_rings = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'g']
G_rings = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'G']
green = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']

print(f"g={len(g_rings)}, G={len(G_rings)}, N={len(green)}")

# Show detailed info about each g ring
for i, gr in enumerate(g_rings):
    rows = [p[0] for p in gr['boundary']]
    cols = [p[1] for p in gr['boundary']]
    center = ((min(rows)+max(rows))/2, (min(cols)+max(cols))/2)
    print(f"  g[{i}]: center={center}, pixels={gr['pixels']}, boundary={gr['boundary']}")

# Show G ring
for gr in G_rings:
    rows = [p[0] for p in gr['boundary']]
    cols = [p[1] for p in gr['boundary']]
    center = ((min(rows)+max(rows))/2, (min(cols)+max(cols))/2)
    print(f"  G: center={center}, pixels={gr['pixels']}")

# Show green objects
for g in green:
    rows = [p[0] for p in g['boundary']]
    cols = [p[1] for p in g['boundary']]
    center = ((min(rows)+max(rows))/2, (min(cols)+max(cols))/2)
    print(f"  N: center={center}, pixels={g['pixels']}")

# Try clicking each gray ring to see which triggers transition
for i, gr in enumerate(g_rings):
    rows = [p[0] for p in gr['boundary']]
    cols = [p[1] for p in gr['boundary']]
    center = ((min(rows)+max(rows))/2, (min(cols)+max(cols))/2)
    r, c = int(center[0]), int(center[1])
    print(f"\nTrying click on g[{i}] at ({r}, {c})")
    action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': r, 'col': c}])
    
    # Check if transition happened
    seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
    colors2 = {}
    for n in seg2['nodes']:
        colors2[n['color']] = colors2.get(n['color'], 0) + 1
    print(f"  After: {colors2}")
    
    # Check for avatar (purple/pink)
    avatar = [n for n in seg2['nodes'] if n['color'] in ['p', 'P', 'M', 'r']]
    if avatar:
        print(f"  AVATAR FOUND!")
        break
    
    # If no change, undo by clicking the green object again
    if colors2 == {'w': 49, 'W': 2, 'S': 1, 'B': 1, 'b': 2, 'G': 1, 'N': 5, 'g': 4}:
        print(f"  No change, need to reset")
        # Click the green object to reset
        action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 19, 'col': 18}])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"MOUSE on green object": "Selects it (G ring), creates g rings", "MOUSE on gray ring (19,12)": "TRANSITION at step 11, but NOT at step 21 - depends on state", "MOUSE on avatar": "REVERSES transition", "Clicking all 5 green objects": "Resets board"}, "object_taxonomy": {"N": "green plus objects", "G": "selection ring", "g": "gray ring markers", "w": "grid cells", "B": "black borders"}, "win_pattern": "Unknown", "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "After clicking green at (19,18): g=4 at cross pattern, G=1 on green, N=5. Clicking (19,12) did NOT trigger transition.", "goal_model": "Need to find correct gray ring to click for transition", "recent_findings": ["Gray rings form cross pattern around (19.5, 30.5)", "Clicking (19,12) didn't trigger transition this time"], "open_questions": ["Which gray ring triggers transition?", "Why did (19,12) work at step 11 but not now?"], "current_plan": "Try clicking each gray ring to find the one that triggers transition", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["Clicking (19,12) with current g ring positions: no transition (step 21)"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
